Page 1

BEARING 315
Newsletter of Cascade Orienteering Club
Jan/Feb 1984

For those curious members wondering why we no longer receive the 'Northwest Orienteer', the executive board voted unanimously to replace it with Cascade's own newsletter to better meet the needs of our members. The other clubs in the region are welcome to send information in to the BEARING 315 staff for consideration. Cascade members wanting to continue receiving the 'Northwest Orienteer' are encouraged to contact Clayton Smith at 474-5675 or write to his box to make arrangements directly with him. Since pictures, see-how-they-run course analyses, and cartoons will not be solicited at present for BEARING 315, some of you might want to receive 'N O', or might want to contact Dan Waugh at 365-1810 for 'Chariots of Fire', the newsletter of the school league. BEARING 315 will contain results, a schedule, an editor's column, and articles of interest contributed by members or selected from other sources; the new editor will try his best to get it to you on time.

Letters to the editor are most welcome and generally will be printed sooner or later, space permitting. Anyone interested in doing layout for the newsletter (particularly cutting and pasting the results together) please contact me. Also volunteers for the mailing socials are needed every 2 months. Any correspondence to Cascade Orienteering Club or to this Newsletter should go to P.O. Box 31375, Seattle, Wash. 98103 Other clubs wishing to receive BEARING 315 in exchange should send their newsletters directly to the above address.

Elections

The annual meeting was held on 14 Dec 1983 with 15 members and 5 guests present. The following were unanimously (including the 3 mailed-in ballots) elected to office: president-Mark Hartinger, vice president-Debbie Newell, secretary-Diane Magasich, and treasurer-Chris Hawkins. Svensk, Per Nordahl, and Norsk, Knut Mathisen, advance men for Peo Bengtson's 1984 U. S. tour, talked a bit on how pleased they were with the arrangements we had made and what we could expect when the tour came this way the last weekend in July. Dan Waugh deserves an award for the effort he spent on gathering information for them. Also present at the meeting was Colin Preston, president of OABC, who spoke of trying to host the 1986 North American championships on a weekend that would mesh with our bid for the 1986 U. S. championships, thereby making both bids more attractive and meaning less work for both hosting organizations. The program was a fine presention of slides of the World championships in Hungary, more than were reprinted in O'USA and in color and with Mike's explanations. At 9:00, a dozen or so of us adjourned to Donna's Homemade Ice Cream shop to discuss more O and to get in our licks.

Page 2

Officers & Board Members

The officers and executive board members of the Cascade Orienteering Club are:

TitleNamePhone

Publicity
Mapping
Instruction
Promotions
Car Pools

The other members of the board will be announced when Mark can select and inform the lucky volunteers. We always need publicity and promotion coordinators so anyone with a little free time and lots of energy is welcome. It is not at all necessary to be a 'top' orienteer---all you need is to enjoy orienteering.

WIOL

The Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League is off to a fine start with over 30 students having participated so far. Having separate registration and separate starts makes for a more pleasant time for regular club members who no longer get lost in the administrative shuffle (but lost on their course, perhaps?). This requires extra help at meets so, as usual, volunteers should contact the vice president.

Peo's Tour

About 50 Scandinavians will be in Seattle for orienteering and sightseeing on the 27-29 July 1984. The tentative schedule will be an afternoon meet on the U. W. campus on Friday, a meet at Crystal Springs on Saturday, and a meet at Lake Tradition in Issaquah on Sunday. Such a schedule requires a lot of work particularly in field-checking to make a map of the quality that the visitors would be used to. Our club treasury does not now have enough money to produce base maps for field checking and color maps for meet use but we hope to get some corporate backing if at all possible. We hope that this event will retain some of our summer members who only enjoy orienteering when they can't be backpacking. Mark your calendars for July as soon as you get them.

1986 U. S. O' Championships

Our bid was announced to the rest of the U. S. O' community in the Nov. 1983 issue of O'USA. Since the initial letter was written, a 6 square mile area east of Duvall was chosen for the competition map. It is very interesting and similar to Norwegian terrain as at the 1982 'SOLO'. We hope to hold a meet there this spring on

Page 3

a U. S. G. S. map and then close it for meets until the championships. So all you good folks out there who have complained about how easy the small parks are or about how we've used the same big areas soooo many times, here is your chance for rough orienteering on a new area. Keep watching for details. (See Colin Preston's comments on OABC's bid to host the 1986 N. A.'s above).

T-shirt design contest?

Many of the old-time (over 2 years) members have club t-shirts; many of the newer members would like to buy club T-shirts. Unfortunately, we no longer have any to sell so it is time to decide whether to have an entirely new design or to reuse the old design. The closing date to submit new designs to a board member is 1 February 1984; members can attend the February club meeting and vote on their favorite design and C.O.C. should have T-shirts for sale by spring again. Or, perhaps, club patches are preferred? Whatever, let us know.

Club dues

Please check the date on the mailing label to see when your membership expires. Renew promptly if you want to continue receiving BEARING 315, the club newsletter, and seeing how your name might be misspelled in the results column. An application form is enclosed somewhere in this newsletter for your convenience and also an application for the U. S. Orienteering Federation. Anyone who travels to other parts of the country and wants to find out about meets there is encouraged to join. Also to retain our charter we need a minimum of 5 USOF members in the club. Words of both thanks and welcome go the following new or returning club members: Elizabeth Anderson, Boy Scout troops 270 and 16, Melisa Bridges, the Sture Carlssons, Neil Cochran, Robert Corliss, Charles Countryman, Ardis Dull, Scott Elich, the Len Englands, the John Howells, Joe Hudson, John Huth, Ken Knapp, the Lewises, Diane Magasich, the Roger Mertens, the Millers, Brad Payne, the Powells, the Saxers, Roger Sharp, Einar Storakers, Valerie Tinsley, Lorna Williamson, and Anne York and Harvey Friedman.

Thanks

We all owe thanks to Jean Hendrickson who is resigning as club treasurer after several years of excellent service and to Harry Morgan, who as vice president and meet coordinator, almost (can't forget Lesley) single-handedly ensured the club holding all the meets that were scheduled for 1983. Let us all try to give the new v. p. more help this year.

Big Brother

On Saturday, Jan 7, COC will hold a special event to welcome Orwell's 1984. Along with the other courses to be set that day in West Seattle's Camp Long (including the 2nd WIOL competition), there will be a "Beat Big Brother" course for the orienteering equivalents of Winston and Julie.
The course will work as follows: A 'freestyle' course will be set (sort of like a score course with no time limit - visit all controls in any order as quickly as

Page 4

possible). A selected controls, however, will be under surveillance by the part apparatus and, because orienteering - like all activities stressing independence - is illegal, punching in at them will be appropriately dealt with. How to avoid this severe action? Well, rebellious agents have obtained photographs of the views seen by the party's surveillance cameras and copies of these photos will be given to each orienteer. To successfully complete the course, each orienteer must punch in at all of the controls on the course except those being watched by Big Brother. Should any orienteer punch at a control that is under surveillance ( a doubleplusungood thing to do) they will be recorded as "no longer existing"- indeed, they will be considered to have never existed.
Experienced orienteers will recognize this as a somewhat practical application of photo-orienteering: attempting to identify a particular control based only on the interpretation of a photograph of it. Those who are proficient in this will not have to visit all of the controls, thereby saving time. Those who get hasty or otherwise err will be sent to the Ministry of Orienteering for retraining. - Mike Schuh

Used sports equipment?

Marsha Miliman (284-8164) plans to open a store soon to sell used sports equipment; phone her if you want to sell through her store on consignment. -Harry M.

Letters to the Editor

Editor, BEARING 315,
Just a note of thanks to the 1983 C.O.C. officers and others in the club who gave so much time to furthering the sport of orienteering. We are again blessed with a very capable, fine group of C.O.C. officers for 1984. Orienteering remains a fun and exciting sport with much promise for the coming year.
Dave Enger

*=unofficial (second course)

Fine weather, interesting terrain, and great courses made this year's Washington State Championships a highly enjoyable experience for those participating. Saturday's courses by Harvey Friedman at the Durr Road "desert" on Manastash Ridge south of Ellensburg provided an unusual change from most western Washington brush bashes. With no tree > 5' but much wide-open space and physical relief, the courses were long and demanding (up to European standards in length). Although the decision was made not to count the Blue course on Day 1, it was based on the standpoint that the map was weak and unclear in a particular area rather than on an error in marker hanging. Day 2 on the familiar Cle Elum Ridge map with courses by Anne York again proved enjoyable, however with much easier running. After a protest by a disgruntled competitor, Anne successfully stood her ground and won the case. Gracious hospitality was again provided by the Flying Horseshoe Ranch and George and Penny Blackburn. Thanks to the many who helped out at one time or another during the weekend and particularly to Harvey and Anne for their long hours.

Page 7

Woodland Park Night Meet 5 November 1983 Volunteer 24/9/1983

Miserable weather failed to dampen the enthusiasm of all the intrepid orienteers that turned out for this very wet event. In addition to the driving rain, 4 separate courses (from 1.3 to 1.6km in length) designed by Ingemar Jansson provided the evening's challenge. A fire, hot cider, and goodies offered some consolation for all those in need afterward.

A small turnout, but several new faces provided interest at this meet. Ingemar Jansson, a UW student and experienced orienteer from Sweden, served notice that he'll be a strong contender in all future events. After running several miles uphill to the park from the UW, he proceeded to ace the memory course on a map and area he had never seen before.

St. Edward State Park 26/12/83

The sun shone bright on the 4th annual 'BOG SLOG', held Dec. 26th at St.Edward State Park. Over 30 hardy souls braved the chill Dec. winds and courses designed by Mike Schuh to vie for prizes of delicious homemade pies. Many thanks to meet helpers Ardis Dull, Dot Newell, Arnold Kay, and Kathy Newell; and thanks also to those little old piemakers Dan Waugh and Anne York.

  • Debbie Newell

SCHEDULE OF EVENTS

GENERAL INFORMATION: Unless otherwise noted, novice, intermediate and advanced courses are available at all club meets. Normal registration and start times are from 10:00 AM to NOON and entry fee is usually $1.50 for members and $2.50 for non-members. Regional and National (Class 'B' and Class 'A') events may have a higher entry fee depending upon sanctions and awards given to winners in various categories. Instruction for beginners is always available at all events. A limited number of compasses are available on a rental basis at registration.

Saturday, 7 Jan --- Camp Long 1:3000 color
The first meet on the new color map of Camp Long; also a chance to cross the new West Seattle bridge for those of you who didn't yet. Beat Big Brother. 5200 35th SW

Saturday, 14 Jan --- Farrel-McWhirter Park 1:3500 color
See how many of the permanent markers are still there. NE 102nd, Redmond

block_index.text: BEARING 315 Newsletter of Cascade Orienteering Club Jan/Feb 1984

For those curious members wondering why we no longer receive the 'Northwest Orienteer', the executive board voted unanimously to replace it with Cascade's own newsletter to better meet the needs of our members. The other clubs in the region are welcome to send information in to the BEARING 315 staff for consideration. Cascade members wanting to continue receiving the 'Northwest Orienteer' are encouraged to contact Clayton Smith at 474-5675 or write to his box to make arrangements directly with him. Since pictures, see-how-they-run course analyses, and cartoons will not be solicited at present for BEARING 315, some of you might want to receive 'N O', or might want to contact Dan Waugh at 365-1810 for 'Chariots of Fire', the newsletter of the school league. BEARING 315 will contain results, a schedule, an editor's column, and articles of interest contributed by members or selected from other sources; the new editor will try his best to get it to you on time.

Letters to the editor are most welcome and generally will be printed sooner or later, space permitting. Anyone interested in doing layout for the newsletter (particularly cutting and pasting the results together) please contact me. Also volunteers for the mailing socials are needed every 2 months. Any correspondence to Cascade Orienteering Club or to this Newsletter should go to P.O. Box 31375, Seattle, Wash. 98103 Other clubs wishing to receive BEARING 315 in exchange should send their newsletters directly to the above address.

The annual meeting was held on 14 Dec 1983 with 15 members and 5 guests present. The following were unanimously (including the 3 mailed-in ballots) elected to office: president-Mark Hartinger, vice president-Debbie Newell, secretary-Diane Magasich, and treasurer-Chris Hawkins. Svensk, Per Nordahl, and Norsk, Knut Mathisen, advance men for Peo Bengtson's 1984 U. S. tour, talked a bit on how pleased they were with the arrangements we had made and what we could expect when the tour came this way the last weekend in July. Dan Waugh deserves an award for the effort he spent on gathering information for them. Also present at the meeting was Colin Preston, president of OABC, who spoke of trying to host the 1986 North American championships on a weekend that would mesh with our bid for the 1986 U. S. championships, thereby making both bids more attractive and meaning less work for both hosting organizations. The program was a fine presention of slides of the World championships in Hungary, more than were reprinted in O'USA and in color and with Mike's explanations. At 9:00, a dozen or so of us adjourned to Donna's Homemade Ice Cream shop to discuss more O and to get in our licks.
The officers and executive board members of the Cascade Orienteering Club are:

The other members of the board will be announced when Mark can select and inform the lucky volunteers. We always need publicity and promotion coordinators so anyone with a little free time and lots of energy is welcome. It is not at all necessary to be a 'top' orienteer---all you need is to enjoy orienteering.

The Washington Interscholastic Orienteering League is off to a fine start with over 30 students having participated so far. Having separate registration and separate starts makes for a more pleasant time for regular club members who no longer get lost in the administrative shuffle (but lost on their course, perhaps?). This requires extra help at meets so, as usual, volunteers should contact the vice president.

About 50 Scandinavians will be in Seattle for orienteering and sightseeing on the 27-29 July 1984. The tentative schedule will be an afternoon meet on the U. W. campus on Friday, a meet at Crystal Springs on Saturday, and a meet at Lake Tradition in Issaquah on Sunday. Such a schedule requires a lot of work particularly in field-checking to make a map of the quality that the visitors would be used to. Our club treasury does not now have enough money to produce base maps for field checking and color maps for meet use but we hope to get some corporate backing if at all possible. We hope that this event will retain some of our summer members who only enjoy orienteering when they can't be backpacking. Mark your calendars for July as soon as you get them.

Our bid was announced to the rest of the U. S. O' community in the Nov. 1983 issue of O'USA. Since the initial letter was written, a 6 square mile area east of Duvall was chosen for the competition map. It is very interesting and similar to Norwegian terrain as at the 1982 'SOLO'. We hope to hold a meet there this spring on

a U. S. G. S. map and then close it for meets until the championships. So all you good folks out there who have complained about how easy the small parks are or about how we've used the same big areas soooo many times, here is your chance for rough orienteering on a new area. Keep watching for details. (See Colin Preston's comments on OABC's bid to host the 1986 N. A.'s above).

Many of the old-time (over 2 years) members have club t-shirts; many of the newer members would like to buy club T-shirts. Unfortunately, we no longer have any to sell so it is time to decide whether to have an entirely new design or to reuse the old design. The closing date to submit new designs to a board member is 1 February 1984; members can attend the February club meeting and vote on their favorite design and C.O.C. should have T-shirts for sale by spring again. Or, perhaps, club patches are preferred? Whatever, let us know.

Please check the date on the mailing label to see when your membership expires. Renew promptly if you want to continue receiving BEARING 315, the club newsletter, and seeing how your name might be misspelled in the results column. An application form is enclosed somewhere in this newsletter for your convenience and also an application for the U. S. Orienteering Federation. Anyone who travels to other parts of the country and wants to find out about meets there is encouraged to join. Also to retain our charter we need a minimum of 5 USOF members in the club. Words of both thanks and welcome go the following new or returning club members: Elizabeth Anderson, Boy Scout troops 270 and 16, Melisa Bridges, the Sture Carlssons, Neil Cochran, Robert Corliss, Charles Countryman, Ardis Dull, Scott Elich, the Len Englands, the John Howells, Joe Hudson, John Huth, Ken Knapp, the Lewises, Diane Magasich, the Roger Mertens, the Millers, Brad Payne, the Powells, the Saxers, Roger Sharp, Einar Storakers, Valerie Tinsley, Lorna Williamson, and Anne York and Harvey Friedman.

We all owe thanks to Jean Hendrickson who is resigning as club treasurer after several years of excellent service and to Harry Morgan, who as vice president and meet coordinator, almost (can't forget Lesley) single-handedly ensured the club holding all the meets that were scheduled for 1983. Let us all try to give the new v. p. more help this year.

On Saturday, Jan 7, COC will hold a special event to welcome Orwell's 1984. Along with the other courses to be set that day in West Seattle's Camp Long (including the 2nd WIOL competition), there will be a "Beat Big Brother" course for the orienteering equivalents of Winston and Julie. The course will work as follows: A 'freestyle' course will be set (sort of like a score course with no time limit - visit all controls in any order as quickly as

possible). A selected controls, however, will be under surveillance by the part apparatus and, because orienteering - like all activities stressing independence - is illegal, punching in at them will be appropriately dealt with. How to avoid this severe action? Well, rebellious agents have obtained photographs of the views seen by the party's surveillance cameras and copies of these photos will be given to each orienteer. To successfully complete the course, each orienteer must punch in at all of the controls on the course except those being watched by Big Brother. Should any orienteer punch at a control that is under surveillance ( a doubleplusungood thing to do) they will be recorded as "no longer existing"- indeed, they will be considered to have never existed. Experienced orienteers will recognize this as a somewhat practical application of photo-orienteering: attempting to identify a particular control based only on the interpretation of a photograph of it. Those who are proficient in this will not have to visit all of the controls, thereby saving time. Those who get hasty or otherwise err will be sent to the Ministry of Orienteering for retraining. - Mike Schuh
Marsha Miliman (284-8164) plans to open a store soon to sell used sports equipment; phone her if you want to sell through her store on consignment. -Harry M.

Editor, BEARING 315, Just a note of thanks to the 1983 C.O.C. officers and others in the club who gave so much time to furthering the sport of orienteering. We are again blessed with a very capable, fine group of C.O.C. officers for 1984. Orienteering remains a fun and exciting sport with much promise for the coming year. Dave Enger
LINCOLN PARK - COC event; Practice Meet for WIOL November 19, 1983
Course Setter - Chris Hawkins Meet Director - Harry Morgan Meet Assistance - Sandy and Robert Miller, Harvey Friedman, John Huth, and others. Weather - Foul, foul, foul!
Washington State Championships 8-9 October 1983 Durr Road Cle Elum Total

Fine weather, interesting terrain, and great courses made this year's Washington State Championships a highly enjoyable experience for those participating. Saturday's courses by Harvey Friedman at the Durr Road "desert" on Manastash Ridge south of Ellensburg provided an unusual change from most western Washington brush bashes. With no tree > 5' but much wide-open space and physical relief, the courses were long and demanding (up to European standards in length). Although the decision was made not to count the Blue course on Day 1, it was based on the standpoint that the map was weak and unclear in a particular area rather than on an error in marker hanging. Day 2 on the familiar Cle Elum Ridge map with courses by Anne York again proved enjoyable, however with much easier running. After a protest by a disgruntled competitor, Anne successfully stood her ground and won the case. Gracious hospitality was again provided by the Flying Horseshoe Ranch and George and Penny Blackburn. Thanks to the many who helped out at one time or another during the weekend and particularly to Harvey and Anne for their long hours.
Woodland Park Night Meet 5 November 1983 Volunteer 24/9/1983

Miserable weather failed to dampen the enthusiasm of all the intrepid orienteers that turned out for this very wet event. In addition to the driving rain, 4 separate courses (from 1.3 to 1.6km in length) designed by Ingemar Jansson provided the evening's challenge. A fire, hot cider, and goodies offered some consolation for all those in need afterward.

A small turnout, but several new faces provided interest at this meet. Ingemar Jansson, a UW student and experienced orienteer from Sweden, served notice that he'll be a strong contender in all future events. After running several miles uphill to the park from the UW, he proceeded to ace the memory course on a map and area he had never seen before.
Orange (4.1 km., 17 controls, white & yellow combined)
ROBINSWOOD PARK - WIOL Practice Meet Sammamish Orienteering Club 12/4/83

Course setting - John Huth Meet director - Diane Magasich Meet assistance - Harvey Friedman, Brad Payne, Harry Morgan, Leslie Morgan, Dan Waugh, Mark Hartinger, Mike Schuh, and others. Post Meet Reception - Carol Johnson Weather - a window of sun before the deluge!

The first of the school league meets was a success, with 41 participants. Separating registration and starts for WIOL worked well and cut down on waiting for others. Special thanks to Carol for opening her apt. to all and to Diane for success as a rookie meet director.

The sun shone bright on the 4th annual 'BOG SLOG', held Dec. 26th at St.Edward State Park. Over 30 hardy souls braved the chill Dec. winds and courses designed by Mike Schuh to vie for prizes of delicious homemade pies. Many thanks to meet helpers Ardis Dull, Dot Newell, Arnold Kay, and Kathy Newell; and thanks also to those little old piemakers Dan Waugh and Anne York.

GENERAL INFORMATION: Unless otherwise noted, novice, intermediate and advanced courses are available at all club meets. Normal registration and start times are from 10:00 AM to NOON and entry fee is usually $1.50 for members and $2.50 for non-members. Regional and National (Class 'B' and Class 'A') events may have a higher entry fee depending upon sanctions and awards given to winners in various categories. Instruction for beginners is always available at all events. A limited number of compasses are available on a rental basis at registration.
